elements.sent-2411-001: Complex numbers are widely used in quantum physics and are indispensable components for describing quantum systems and their dynamical behavior.
elements.sent-2411-002: The resource theory of imaginarity has been built recently, enabling a systematic research of complex numbers in quantum information theory.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this work, we develop two theoretical methods for quantifying imaginarity, motivated by recent progress within resource theories of entanglement and coherence.
elements.sent-2411-004: We provide quantifiers of imaginarity by the convex roof construction and quantifiers of the imaginarity by the least imaginarity of the input pure states under real operations.
elements.sent-2411-005: We also apply these tools to study the state conversion problem in resource theory of imaginarity.
elements.sent-2411-006: Quantum mechanics is traditionally formulated using complex numbers.
elements.sent-2411-007: Hickey and Gour \cite{Hickey1 introduced the imaginarity of a quantum state as a quantum resource theory \cite{Hickey2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-008: In spite of this rigorous resource theoretic treatment, its usefulness is initially unclear.
elements.sent-2411-009: Indeed, there has been a long-standing debate since the inception of quantum mechanics about why quantum mechanics uses complex numbers rather than solely real numbers.
elements.sent-2411-010: Some early results in the literature suggest that imaginarity has no relevant role, as, for instance, various real-only formulations of quantum mechanics were known \cite{Stueckelberg1,Stueckelberg2,Wootters1,Wootters2,Aleksandrova,Wootters3 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Surprisingly, Renou et al. recently have found an experimental scenario that cannot be exactly modeled using quantum theory with real-valued amplitudes only \cite{Renou .}
elements.sent-2411-012: These findings in relevant scenarios have been experimentally verified and proposed \cite{Fan,Pan1,Pan2,Bednorz,Fan2 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: From then on, there is a burst of investigations on the imaginarity resource theory.
elements.sent-2411-014: It is shown that imaginarity as an important resource have crucial effects in certain discrimination tasks \cite{Guo1 , hiding and masking \cite{Zhu , multiparameter metrology \cite{Miyazaki , machine learning \cite{Sajjan , pseudorandomness \cite{Haug , outcome statistics of linear-optical experiments \cite{Jones , Kirkwood-Dirac quasiprobability distributions \cite{Budiyono1,Budiyono2,Budiyono3,Wagner , weak-value theory \cite{Wagner2 and nonlocal advantages of quantum imaginarity \cite{Fei .}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: A resource theory is fundamentally determined by two basic elements: free states and free operations \cite{Hickey2,Adesso .}
elements.sent-2411-016: The free states in resource theory of imaginarity are {\it real states , i.e., quantum states with a real density matrix $\langle m |\rho |n\rangle\in{\mathbb R}$ for a fixed reference basis $\{|m\ra\}_{m=1 ^d$ of $\mathcal H$.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: The free operations are {\it real operations , i.e., quantum operations $\Phi(\rho) =\sum_j K_j\rho K_j^{\dag}$ with real Kraus operators: $\langle m|K_j|n\rangle\in{\mathbb R}$.}
elements.sent-2411-018: One central question in resource theory of imaginarity is quantitative analysis that captures the resource character of physical traits in a mathematically rigorous fashion.
elements.sent-2411-019: A nonnegative function ${\mathcal I}$ on quantum states is called an imaginarity measure if ${\mathcal I}$ satisfies the following conditions (I1) to (I4) \cite{Hickey1,Guo2,Guo1 .}
elements.sent-2411-021: In Ref. \cite{Li , the authors considered the condition (I5) below.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Several imaginarity measures have been found, such as geometric imaginarity \cite{Guo2 , the robustness of imaginarity \cite{Guo2 , the trace norm and $l_1$ norm of imaginarity \cite{Hickey1,Guo1,Gao , the relative entropy of imaginarity \cite{Li , imaginarity based on the fidelity \cite{Guo2,Kondra,Xu and Tsallis relative entropy \cite{Xu2 .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: It is obvious that different quantifications not only provide different computability, but also imply different operational meanings.
elements.sent-2411-024: However, as far as a finite number of imaginarity measures are considered, the quantification of imaginarity is still in early stages.
elements.sent-2411-025: How to explore the new understandings of imaginarity remains still a significant and attractive topic in the resource theory of imaginarity.
elements.sent-2411-026: In this paper, we firstly provides quantifiers of imaginarity by the convex roof construction, a traditional and effective method in the quantification of entanglement \cite{Uhlmann,Bennett,Vidal and coherence \cite{Du1 .}}
elements.sent-2411-027: Based on general properties of our recipe and their connection to the state conversion problem, no-go theorem of imaginarity convertibility on general mixed states are built.
elements.sent-2411-028: Secondly, we consider that some pure states undergo real operations \cite{Guo1 and finally become the common objective state.}
elements.sent-2411-029: It is shown that the imaginarity of the objective state can be well described by the least imaginarity of the pure input states.
elements.sent-2411-030: So a imaginarity monotone is established by only effectively quantifying the input pure states.
elements.sent-2411-031: This automatically endows the imaginarity monotone with an operational meaning.
elements.sent-2411-032: Thirdly, the inner relation between convex roof quantifiers of imaginarity and imaginarity monotone induced by input pure states is revealed in detail.
elements.sent-2411-033: In the first, let us treat convex roof quantifiers of imaginarity.
elements.sent-2411-034: Assume that $f:[0,1]\rightarrow [0,1]$ is a function satisfying the following conditions: (i) $f(1)=0$; (ii) $f$ is decreasing; (iii) $f$ is concave: $f(\lambda {x +(1-\lambda) {y )\geq \lambda f({x )+(1-\lambda)f({y )$ for all $\lambda\in [0,1]$ and all $x,y\in [0,1]$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: For any pure state $|\psi\rangle\langle\psi|$, define \begin{equation}{\mathcal I}_f(|\psi\rangle)=f(|\la \psi^*|\psi\ra|),\end{equation} here $|\psi^*\rangle$ is the complex conjugate of $|\psi\rangle$.
elements.sent-2411-036: For any mixed state $\rho$, define \begin{equation}{\mathcal I}_f(\rho)=\min _{p_i,|\psi_i\rangle}\{\sum_i p_i{\mathcal I}_f(|\psi_i\rangle):\ \rho=\sum\limits_i p_i|\psi_i\rangle\langle\psi_i|\}.\end{equation}. Theorem 1.
elements.sent-2411-037: Conversely, the restriction of any imaginarity measure satisfying {\rm (I1), (I3), (I4)) to pure states is identical to ${\mathcal I _f$ for some $f$.}}
elements.sent-2411-038: It is well-known that both convex roof quantifiers of entanglement \cite{Uhlmann,Bennett,Vidal and convex roof quantifiers of coherence \cite{Du1 are dependent on real symmetric concave functions on probability distributions, while convex roof quantifiers of imaginarity are dependent on monotonically decreasing concave functions on $[0,1]$ from Theorem 1.}}
elements.sent-2411-039: This shows that convex roof quantifiers of imaginarity are more handy for operation.
elements.sent-2411-040: In addition, if $f$ is strictly decreasing, then ${\mathcal I _f$ is faithful, that is, ${\mathcal I _f (\rho) = 0$ if and only if $\rho$ is a real state.}}
elements.sent-2411-041: Using Theorem 1, one can construct many interesting imaginarity measures.
elements.sent-2411-043: Example 1: Let $f(x)=\frac{1-x {2}$, then \begin{equation}\mathcal I_f(|\psi\rangle)=\frac{1-|\psi^*\rangle\langle\psi|}{2}\end{equation} for a pure state $|\psi\rangle$.}
elements.sent-2411-044: For mixed states, $\mathcal I_f$ is defined by convex roof method.
elements.sent-2411-045: Actually, $\mathcal I_f$ equals to the key geometric imaginarity $\mathcal I_G=1-\max_{|\phi\rangle\in \mathcal R |\la\phi|\psi\ra|$ \cite{Guo1,Guo2,Kondra .}}
elements.sent-2411-046: Example 2: Let $f(x)=\sqrt{\frac{1-x^2 {2 }$, assume $\rho=|\psi\rangle\langle\psi|=\sum_{m,n}\rho_{mn |m\rangle\langle n|\ (\rho_{mn =\langle m|\rho |n\rangle)$, a direct computation (see Proposition 9) shows \begin{equation}\mathcal I_f(|\psi\rangle)=\sum_{m\neq n} \sqrt{({\rm Im}\rho_{mn})^2},\end{equation} here ${\rm Im}\rho_{mn}$ denotes the imaginary part of $\rho_{mn}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: This shows the restriction of $\mathcal I_f$ on pure states is imaginarity measures induced by $l_2$ norm.
elements.sent-2411-049: An interesting observation in quantum coherence is that a desirable function induced by $l_2$ norms does not satisfy probabilistic monotonicity \cite{Baumgratz .}
elements.sent-2411-050: By Theorem 1, imaginarity measures for pure states correspond to monotonically decreasing concave functions on $[0,1]$.
elements.sent-2411-051: The corresponding relationships between several well-known imaginarity measures and decreasing concave functions are revealed (see Table I), and so we can induce these measures for pure states by convex roof construction.
elements.sent-2411-052: However, quantifiers of imaginarity by the convex roof construction are indeed different from these known imaginarity measures on mixed states.
elements.sent-2411-053: Let us recall the notations and definitions of these measures.
elements.sent-2411-054: The robustness of imaginarity $\mathcal I_{R}$ \cite{Guo2 , relative entropy of imaginarity \cite{Li , denoted by $\mathcal I_r$, $\mathcal I_F$ based on the fidelity \cite{Guo2,Kondra,Xu and imaginarity measure based on Tsallis relative entropy $\mathcal I_{T,\mu}$ \cite{Xu2 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: They are defined as \begin{equation}\mathcal I_R(\rho)=\min_{\tau}\{s\geq0:\frac{\rho+s\tau}{1+s}\in \mathcal R, \tau\in\mathcal S(H)\},\end{equation} \begin{equation}\mathcal I_r(\rho)=S(\text{Re} \rho)-S(\rho),\end{equation} \begin{equation}\mathcal I_F(\rho)=1-F(\rho,\rho^*),\end{equation} \begin{equation}\mathcal I_{T,\mu}(\rho)=1-\text{Tr}[\rho^{\mu}(\rho^*)^{1-\mu},\end{equation} where $\text{Re (\rho)$ is the real part of $\rho$, $\rho^*$ denotes the complex conjugate of $\rho$, $\mu\in(0,1)$ and $F(\rho,\sigma)=1-\text{Tr}\sqrt{\sqrt{\rho \sigma\sqrt{\rho }$ is the fidelity of $\rho$ and $\sigma$.. \begin{center.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-056: Given two imaginary states $\rho$ and $\sigma$, one of the main questions studied within the resource theory of imaginarity is whether either $\rho$ can be transformed into $\sigma$ or vice versa by real operations, i.e., identifying what extra resources are necessary to enable such transformations \cite{Guo1,Guo2 .}
elements.sent-2411-057: In fact, imaginarity quantification and state convertibility are in fact closely connected.
elements.sent-2411-058: By (I2), if a state $\rho$ can be converted into $\sigma$ via real operations, then \begin{equation}{\mathcal I}(\sigma)\leq {\mathcal I}(\rho)\end{equation} for any imaginarity monotone ${\mathcal I}$.
elements.sent-2411-059: On the other hand, the fact that (9) holds for some imaginarity monotone ${\mathcal I}$ does not guarantee that the transformation $\rho \rightarrow\sigma$ is possible via real operations.
elements.sent-2411-060: The aim of state convertibility is to find a complete set of coherence monotones $\{{\mathcal I _s\}$ which can completely classify states transformation, i.e., \begin{equation}\rho\rightarrow \sigma\Leftrightarrow {\mathcal I}_s(\rho)\geq {\mathcal I}_s(\sigma)\text{\ for all } s. end{equation} In \cite{Guo1 , the authors have answered the question of pure-state convertibility in terms of geometric imaginarity: For pure states $|\phi\rangle, |\psi\rangle$, \begin{equation}|\psi\rangle \xrightarrow{{\rm RO}}|\phi\rangle \quad \text{iff} \quad {\mathcal I}_f(|\phi\rangle)\leq {\mathcal I}_f(|\psi\rangle),\end{equation} here RO denotes real operations and $f(x)=\frac{1-x {2}$.}}}

elements.sent-2411-176: In this work, we have provided two approaches to quantify imaginarity.
elements.sent-2411-177: In the first, we have built the framework of the convex roof construction in resource theory of imaginarity, a traditional and effective method in the quantification of entanglement and coherence.
elements.sent-2411-178: Moreover, it has been shown the restriction of any imaginarity measure satisfying (I1), (I3), (I4)) to pure states is identical to some ${\mathcal I _f $.}
elements.sent-2411-179: Based on this method, one can construct many interesting imaginarity measures, such as the geometric imaginarity and so on.
elements.sent-2411-180: We also apply the tool to study the state conversion problem in resource theory of imaginarity.
elements.sent-2411-181: It is found that there does not exist a finite complete set of imaginarity measures determining mixed-state conversion.
elements.sent-2411-182: Secondly, we present the imaginarity monotone in terms of a state conversion process under real operations.
elements.sent-2411-183: In particular, it is shown that our imaginarity monotone is the supremum of all imaginarity monotones with the same imaginarity for any given pure state.
elements.sent-2411-184: An interesting observation is that the geometric imaginarity can be presented by this approach which provides operational meaning to the geometric imaginarity.
elements.sent-2411-185: The inner relation between quantifiers of imaginarity by the convex roof construction and quantifiers of the imaginarity by the least imagi- narity of the input pure states has also discussed.
elements.sent-2411-186: We acknowledge that this research was supported by NSF of China (12271452), NSF of Xiamen (3502Z202373018), and NSF of Fujian (2023J01028).
elements.sent-2411-187: It is shown that (I3) and (I4) are equivalent to (I2) and (I5) \cite{Li .}
elements.sent-2411-188: In this paper, we firstly provide quantifiers of imaginarity by the convex roof construction, a traditional and effective method in the quantification of entanglement \cite{Uhlmann,Bennett,Vidal and coherence \cite{Du1 .}}

elements.sent-2411-192: They are defined as \begin{equation}\mathcal I_R(\rho)=\min_{\tau}\{s\geq0:\frac{\rho+s\tau}{1+s}\in \mathcal R, \tau\in\mathcal S(H)\},\end{equation} \begin{equation}\mathcal I_r(\rho)=S(\text{Re} \rho)-S(\rho),\end{equation} \begin{equation}\mathcal I_F(\rho)=1-F(\rho,\rho^*),\end{equation} \begin{equation}\mathcal I_{T,\mu}(\rho)=1-\text{Tr}[\rho^{\mu}(\rho^*)]^{1-\mu},\end{equation} where $\text{Re (\rho)$ is the real part of $\rho$, $\rho^*$ denotes the complex conjugate of $\rho$, $\mu\in(0,1)$ and $F(\rho,\sigma)=1-\text{Tr}\sqrt{\sqrt{\rho \sigma\sqrt{\rho }$ is the fidelity of $\rho$ and $\sigma$.. \begin{center.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-196: The inner relation between quantifiers of imaginarity by the convex roof construction and quantifiers of the imaginarity by the least imaginarity of the input pure states has also discussed.
elements.sent-2411-197: A closely related area with quantifying imaginarity is how to detect imaginarity for a tupe of quantum states $(\rho_1, \rho_2, \ldots, \rho_n)$ (see \cite{Wagner3 and references therein).}
elements.sent-2411-198: Our results raise an interesting question how to quantifying imaginarity of $(\rho_1, \rho_2, \ldots, \rho_n)$? It extends the definition of imaginarity measure and may develop new methods for quantifying imaginarity.
elements.sent-2411-199: The authors thank professor Jianwei Xu for helpful comments during the preparation of this paper.

elements.formula-2411-055: \begin{equation}\label{eq4} \left\{ {l} \sum_i p_i |x_i|^2=\frac{\lambda(1+z)}2, \sum_i p_i |y_i|^2=\frac{\lambda(1-z)}2, \sum_i p_i |z_i|^2=1-\lambda, \sum_i p_i x_iy_i^*={\rm i}\frac{\lambda\sqrt{1-z^2}}2.  \right. (A3)}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-209: The saturation of the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality requires $x_i = gy_i$ for any $i$, with $g$ being a nonzero constant.
elements.sent-2411-210: Thus it is not difficult to find $g={\rm i}\sqrt{\frac{1+z {1-z }$ from (A3).}}
elements.sent-2411-211: So \begin{widetext \begin{equation}{ll} \sum_i p_i|\la\phi^*_i|\phi_i\rangle| = \sum_i p_i\sqrt{1-4(( Im(x_iy_i^*))^2+( Im(x_iz_i^*))^2+( Im(y_iz_i^*))^2)} = \sum_i p_i\sqrt{1-4(|g|^2|y_i|^4+|g|^2( Re(y_iz_i^*))^2+( Im(y_iz_i^*))^2)} \leq \sum_i p_i\sqrt{1-4(|g|^2|y_i|^4+|y_i|^2|z_i|^2)} = \sum_i p_i\sqrt{(|x_i|^2+|y_i|^2+|z_i|^2)^2-4(|g|^2|y_i|^4+|y_i|^2|z_i|^2)} = \sum_i p_i\sqrt{((|g|^2+1)|y_i|^2+|z_i|^2)^2-4(|g|^2|y_i|^4+|y_i|^2|z_i|^2)} = \sum_i p_i\sqrt{((|g|^2-1)|y_i|^2+|z_i|^2)^2} = \sum_i p_i((|g|^2-1)|y_i|^2+|z_i|^2) = \lambda z+1-\lambda, \end{equation}\end{widetext where the first equality is from (A2).}}
elements.sent-2411-212: It is easy to check that $\Phi(\cdot)=K_1\cdot K_1^{\dag +K_2\cdot K_2^{\dag}$ is a real operation.}
elements.sent-2411-213: And \begin{equation}K_1|\phi\ra=\sqrt{\lambda}|\psi_1\ra,\ \ \ K_2|\phi\ra=\sqrt{1-\lambda}|\psi_2\ra.\ \ \ (A4)\end{equation} From (I3 Probabilistic monotonicity), we get that \begin{equation}\lambda \mu(|\psi_1\ra)+(1-\lambda) \mu( | \psi_2\rangle) \leq \mu (|\phi\ra). \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ (A5)\end{equation} That is $f(\lambda { x +(1-\lambda ){ y )\geq \lambda f({ x )+(1-\lambda)f({ y )$, i.e., $f$ is concave.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-214: From Proposition 2, there is real pure state $|\psi\ra $ and real operation $\Phi$ such that $\Phi(|\psi\ra)=\rho$.
elements.sent-2411-215: Suppose $|\psi\ra\in {\mathfrak R (\rho)$ is the optimal pure state subject to $\widetilde{\mathcal I _f (\rho) =\widetilde{\mathcal I _f(|\psi\ra)$, then it implies that $\Phi_0(|\psi\ra\la\psi|)=\rho$ for some real operation $\Phi_0$.. Then $\Phi(\rho)=\Phi(\Phi_0(|\psi\ra))$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-216: Let \begin{equation}p_n=TrK_n\rho K_n^{\dag},\end{equation} \begin{equation}\rho_n=\frac{K_n\rho K_n^{\dag}}{p_n},\end{equation} and $|\psi\ra$ be an optimal state in ${\mathfrak R (\rho)$ such that $\widetilde{ {\mathcal I _f(\rho)={\mathcal I _f(|\psi\ra)$, $\rho=\sum_jA_j|\psi\ra\la\psi|A_j^{\dag}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-217: Introducing the quantity $c_{nj =\la\psi |K_n^TA^{T _ jA_jK_n|\psi\ra$, we obtain \begin{equation}{ll} \sum_n p_n \widetilde{ {\mathcal I}}_f(\frac{K_n \rho K_n^{\dag}}{p_n}) = \sum_n p_n \widetilde{ {\mathcal I}}_f(\sum_j \frac{K_nA_j |\psi\ra\la \psi|A_j^{\dag} K_n^{\dag}}{p_n}) = \sum_n p_n \widetilde{{\mathcal I}}_f(\sum_j \frac {c_{nj}}{p_n}\times \frac{K_nA_j |\psi\ra\la \psi| A_j^{\dag}K_n^{\dag}}{c_{nj}}) \leq \sum_{n} p_nf(\frac {\sum _j |\la K_n^*A_j^* \psi^*|\psi A_jK_n\ra| }{p_{n}}) \leq f(\sum_{nj} |\la K_n^*A_j^* \psi^*|\psi A_jK_n\ra| ) \leq f(|\la \psi^*|\psi\ra|)=\widetilde{\mathcal I}_f(|\psi\ra),\end{equation} where the first inequality is from the definition of $\widetilde{ {\mathcal I _f$ and Proposition 2, the second inequality is because of the concavity of $f$, the third comes from Proposition 1 and decreasing property of $f$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-218: For each pure ensemble $\{p_i, |\phi_i\ra\}$, using Proposition 2, we obtain that \begin{equation}\widetilde{\mathcal I}_f(\rho) \leq \min_{\{p_i, |\phi_i\ra\}}f (\sum_i p_i|\la\phi^*_i|\phi_i\rangle|).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-219: Example 2: Let $f(x)=\sqrt{\frac{1-x^2 {2 }$, assume $\rho=|\psi\rangle\langle\psi|=\sum_{m,n}\rho_{mn |m\rangle\langle n|\ (\rho_{mn =\langle m|\rho |n\rangle)$, a direct computation (see Proposition 3 in the appendix) shows \begin{equation}\mathcal I_f(|\psi\rangle)=\sqrt{\sum_{m\neq n}({\rm Im}\rho_{mn})^2},\end{equation} here ${\rm Im}\rho_{mn}$ denotes the imaginary part of $\rho_{mn}$.}}}}
